title: Check Whether PostgreSQL Is Accepting Connections
hook: The database was running, but it was not ready.
problem: An app cannot reach PostgreSQL and you need a quick first-response check before digging into logs or credentials.
starting_state: A host may have PostgreSQL listening locally.
what_changed: Nothing changes. pg_isready reports whether the server accepts connections.
when_to_use: Use during deploys, incident triage, service restarts, and health checks.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat readiness as proof that a specific user, database, or query works.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A line showing host, port, and whether PostgreSQL is accepting connections.

shorts.title: Check Postgres readiness first.
shorts.voiceover: Before changing configs, ask PostgreSQL whether it is accepting connections on the expected host and port.
shorts.caption: Readiness is the first split.

linkedin.question: When an app cannot reach Postgres, do you check readiness before logs?

youtube.description: Use pg_isready to check whether PostgreSQL is accepting connections.

experiments.variant_a: The database was running, but it was not ready.
experiments.variant_b: Check Postgres readiness before changing config.

title: Show Active PostgreSQL Connections
hook: The database was not down. It was full.
problem: PostgreSQL is slow or rejecting clients and you need to see current sessions quickly.
command: psql -X -A -F '|' -c "select pid,usename,datname,state,client_addr from pg_stat_activity order by state, pid;"
starting_state: PostgreSQL is reachable and pg_stat_activity can be queried.
what_changed: Nothing changes. psql prints active backend sessions.
when_to_use: Use when apps report connection exhaustion, slow requests, or stuck database access.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this to terminate sessions; it only lists them.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Rows showing PID, user, database, state, and client address.

commands: psql -X -A -F '|' -c "select pid,usename,datname,state,client_addr from pg_stat_activity order by state, pid;"
psql -X -c "select state, count(*) from pg_stat_activity group by state order by state;"

shorts.title: List Postgres sessions fast.
shorts.voiceover: When PostgreSQL feels unavailable, check whether it is out of connection room before restarting anything.
shorts.caption: pg_stat_activity shows the crowd.

linkedin.question: Do you check active database sessions before restarting PostgreSQL?

youtube.description: Query pg_stat_activity to inspect active PostgreSQL connections.

experiments.variant_a: The database was not down. It was full.

title: Find Long-Running PostgreSQL Queries
hook: One query can make the whole app look broken.
problem: PostgreSQL is slow and you need to identify queries that have been running the longest.
command: psql -X -c "select pid, now() - query_start as age, state, left(query, 80) as query from pg_stat_activity where query_start is not null order by age desc limit 10;"
starting_state: PostgreSQL is reachable and activity statistics are available.
what_changed: Nothing changes. PostgreSQL returns a sorted list of long-running activity.
when_to_use: Use during slow incidents before killing processes or restarting services.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every long query is bad; backups, migrations, and reports may be expected.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A top-10 list of backend PIDs, query age, state, and query prefix.

commands: psql -X -c "select pid, now() - query_start as age, state, left(query, 80) as query from pg_stat_activity where query_start is not null order by age desc limit 10;"
psql -X -c "select state, count(*) from pg_stat_activity group by state;"

shorts.title: Find the slow query first.
shorts.voiceover: Before blaming the whole database, sort active queries by age and see what has been running the longest.
shorts.caption: Start with the oldest query.

linkedin.question: What is your first command when PostgreSQL suddenly feels slow?

youtube.description: Find long-running PostgreSQL queries with pg_stat_activity.

experiments.variant_a: One query can make the whole app look broken.
experiments.variant_b: Sort Postgres activity by query age.

title: Check PostgreSQL Lock Waits
hook: The outage was a queue, not a crash.
problem: Writes are stuck and you need to see whether sessions are waiting on locks.
command: psql -X -c "select pid, wait_event_type, wait_event, state, left(query, 80) as query from pg_stat_activity where wait_event_type is not null order by pid;"
starting_state: PostgreSQL is reachable and sessions may be waiting on locks or IO.
what_changed: Nothing changes. PostgreSQL prints sessions with wait events.
when_to_use: Use when requests hang, migrations stall, or writes stop moving.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this alone to decide which backend to terminate.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Rows showing waiting PIDs, wait event type, wait event, state, and query prefix.

commands: psql -X -c "select pid, wait_event_type, wait_event, state, left(query, 80) as query from pg_stat_activity where wait_event_type is not null order by pid;"
psql -X -c "select wait_event_type, count(*) from pg_stat_activity where wait_event_type is not null group by wait_event_type;"

shorts.title: Check for lock waits.
shorts.voiceover: A stuck database may be waiting behind one lock. Query wait events before you restart PostgreSQL.
shorts.caption: Wait events explain the stall.

linkedin.question: Do you check wait events before restarting a stuck database?

youtube.description: Inspect PostgreSQL wait events during stuck writes or slow incidents.

experiments.variant_a: The outage was a queue, not a crash.
experiments.variant_b: Check Postgres wait events before restart.

title: Show PostgreSQL Database Sizes
hook: Disk pressure starts with knowing what grew.
problem: Disk usage is rising and you need a quick database-level size breakdown.
command: psql -X -c "select datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) as size from pg_database order by pg_database_size(datname) desc;"
starting_state: PostgreSQL is reachable and size functions can be queried.
what_changed: Nothing changes. PostgreSQL prints database names and sizes.
when_to_use: Use when storage alerts fire or backups suddenly take longer.
when_not_to_use: Do not use database size alone to identify the exact table or index responsible.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of database names and human-readable sizes.

commands: psql -X -c "select datname, pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) as size from pg_database order by pg_database_size(datname) desc;"
psql -X -c "select datname from pg_database order by datname;"

shorts.title: Find the biggest Postgres DB.
shorts.voiceover: When disk alerts fire, start with database sizes before hunting individual tables.
shorts.caption: Size the database first.

linkedin.question: When database storage grows, do you check database sizes before tables?

youtube.description: List PostgreSQL database sizes during disk pressure triage.

experiments.variant_a: Disk pressure starts with knowing what grew.
experiments.variant_b: Find the biggest Postgres database first.

title: Check Whether MySQL Responds
hook: The port was open. MySQL still had to answer.
problem: An app cannot connect to MySQL and you need to distinguish a dead server from a credential or query issue.
starting_state: A host may have MySQL listening locally.
what_changed: Nothing changes. mysqladmin reports whether the server responds.
when_to_use: Use during deploys, restarts, and first-response database checks.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat ping success as proof that app credentials or schema permissions are correct.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A short response such as mysqld is alive.

shorts.title: Ping MySQL before guessing.
shorts.voiceover: An open port is not the same as a working database. Ask MySQL whether it is alive.
shorts.caption: mysqladmin ping is the split.

linkedin.question: Do you separate MySQL reachability from credential checks during incidents?

youtube.description: Use mysqladmin ping to check whether MySQL responds.

experiments.variant_a: The port was open. MySQL still had to answer.
experiments.variant_b: Ping MySQL before changing app config.

title: Show Active MySQL Sessions
hook: The app was waiting behind busy sessions.
problem: MySQL is slow or rejecting clients and you need a quick view of active sessions.
starting_state: MySQL is reachable and the current user can view process information.
what_changed: Nothing changes. MySQL prints current sessions and queries.
when_to_use: Use when MySQL feels saturated, slow, or overloaded.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this as the kill step; inspect carefully before terminating sessions.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Rows showing connection ID, user, host, database, command, time, state, and query text.

shorts.title: List MySQL sessions.
shorts.voiceover: Before restarting MySQL, inspect the process list and see who is connected and what they are doing.
shorts.caption: Processlist before restart.

linkedin.question: What do you check before restarting a slow MySQL server?

youtube.description: Use show full processlist to inspect active MySQL sessions.

experiments.variant_a: The app was waiting behind busy sessions.

title: Find Long-Running MySQL Queries
hook: One old query explained the whole slowdown.
problem: MySQL is slow and you need to identify queries that have been running the longest.
command: mysql -e "select id,user,host,db,command,time,state,left(info,80) as info from information_schema.processlist where command <> 'Sleep' order by time desc limit 10;"
starting_state: MySQL is reachable and information_schema.processlist is available.
what_changed: Nothing changes. MySQL prints active non-sleeping sessions sorted by age.
when_to_use: Use during latency spikes, stuck reports, and migration incidents.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every long-running query is unsafe; verify owner and purpose first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of active MySQL sessions with runtime and query prefix.

commands: mysql -e "select id,user,host,db,command,time,state,left(info,80) as info from information_schema.processlist where command <> 'Sleep' order by time desc limit 10;"
mysql -e "select command, count(*) from information_schema.processlist group by command;"

shorts.title: Find old MySQL queries.
shorts.voiceover: When MySQL slows down, sort active sessions by runtime before you blame the entire server.
shorts.caption: Oldest active query first.

linkedin.question: Do you sort MySQL sessions by runtime during slow incidents?

youtube.description: Find long-running MySQL queries using information_schema.processlist.

experiments.variant_a: One old query explained the whole slowdown.

title: Show MySQL Database Sizes
hook: The storage alert needed a database name.
problem: Disk usage is rising and you need to know which MySQL schemas are largest.
command: mysql -e "select table_schema, round(sum(data_length + index_length)/1024/1024, 1) as mb from information_schema.tables group by table_schema order by mb desc;"
starting_state: MySQL is reachable and information_schema.tables can be queried.
what_changed: Nothing changes. MySQL prints schema sizes.
when_to_use: Use when storage alerts fire, backups grow, or migrations unexpectedly increase disk usage.
when_not_to_use: Do not use schema size alone to identify the exact table or index growth source.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of schema names and approximate size in MiB.

commands: mysql -e "select table_schema, round(sum(data_length + index_length)/1024/1024, 1) as mb from information_schema.tables group by table_schema order by mb desc;"

shorts.title: Find the biggest MySQL schema.
shorts.voiceover: When MySQL storage grows, get the schema-level view before drilling into individual tables.
shorts.caption: Schema sizes first.

linkedin.question: When MySQL disk usage grows, do you check schema sizes first?

youtube.description: List MySQL schema sizes from information_schema.tables.

experiments.variant_a: The storage alert needed a database name.
experiments.variant_b: Find the largest MySQL schema first.

title: Check Whether Databases Listen Publicly
hook: The fastest database security check is the listening address.
problem: You need to know whether PostgreSQL or MySQL is exposed beyond localhost.
starting_state: A Linux host may have PostgreSQL or MySQL listening on local or public interfaces.
what_changed: Nothing changes. ss and awk print matching listening sockets.
when_to_use: Use during hardening, breach triage, VPS audits, and deploy reviews.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat localhost binding as complete security; credentials, firewall rules, and tunnels still matter.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Listening socket rows for ports 5432 and 3306, including bind addresses.

shorts.title: Check database exposure.
shorts.voiceover: Before touching credentials, check whether Postgres or MySQL is listening on a public interface.
shorts.caption: Bind address tells the story.

linkedin.question: Do you check database bind addresses during VPS security reviews?

youtube.description: Use ss to inspect whether PostgreSQL or MySQL listens publicly.

experiments.variant_a: The fastest database security check is the listening address.
experiments.variant_b: Check whether your database is listening publicly.
